title: From Tensor Networks to Tractable Circuits, and back
authors: Arend-Jan Quist, Marc Farreras Bartra, Alexis de Colnet, John van de Wetering, Alfons Laarman
abstract: Tensor networks and circuits are widely used data structures to represent pseudo-Boolean functions. These two formalisms have been studied primarily in separate communities, and this paper aims to establish equivalences between them. We show that some classes of tensor networks that are appealing in practice correspond to classes of circuits with specific properties that have been studied in knowledge compilation as \emph{tractable circuits}. In particular, we prove that matrix product states (tensor trains) coincide with nondeterministic edge-valued decision diagrams and that tree tensor networks exactly correspond to structured-decomposable circuits. These correspondences enable direct transfer of structural and algorithmic results; for example, canonicity and tractability guarantees known for circuits yield analogous guarantees for the associated tensor networks, and vice versa.
summary: This paper bridges the gap between tensor networks and knowledge compilation by proving they are mathematically equivalent representations of the same class of functions. This allows physicists to use advanced algorithmic tools from formal verification, such as variable reordering and canonical forms, to optimize tensor network computations.
main_problem: Establishing formal mathematical equivalences between the Tensor Network community (used in physics/ML) and the Tractable Circuits community (used in formal verification) to enable the transfer of algorithms and structural properties.
main_result: The paper proves that Matrix Product States (Tensor Trains) are equivalent to non-deterministic edge-valued decision diagrams, and Tree Tensor Networks correspond to structured-decomposable circuits, allowing for bidirectional transfer of complexity results and heuristics.
method: The authors use the language of semi-rings to unify weights and develop constructive, polynomial-time transformation procedures between the two frameworks.
model_or_system: The paper focuses on mathematical data structures for representing pseudo-Boolean functions, specifically Matrix Product States (MPS), Tree Tensor Networks (TTN), and Edge-Valued Decision Diagrams (EVDD).
main_assumptions: The transformation from EVDD to TT assumes EVDDs are complete (all variables are read on every path), though this can be enforced efficiently.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the structural mapping between a structured-decomposable circuit and a tree tensor network; Figure 2 demonstrates the functional equivalence between an nd-EVDD and a Tensor Train.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of isolated frameworks, defines the mathematical models for both sides using semi-rings, proves specific structural equivalences (TT to EVDD and TTN to circuits), provides complexity analysis for transformations, and discusses the implications for cross-domain utility like variable ordering and lower bounds.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Example of a structured-decomposable circuit (left) and its corresponding tree tensor network (right). The matrices of the tensors correspond to adjacency matrices between the layers of the circuit.

Figure 2: A non-deterministic edge-valued decision diagram (nd-EVDD) and its equivalence to a tensor train (TT). A pseudo-Boolean function f(x1, . . . , x4) is encoded as an nd-EVDD, which is structurally equivalent to a TT representation. The red edges highlight the bipartite subgraph between levels 2 and 3 of the diagram, whose adjacency matrix directly corresponds to one of the tensors in the TT. Dashed and black double arrows indicate how this bipartite graph decomposes into two subgraphs: one for low edges and one for high edges, encoding the matrices A(2) 0 and A(2) 1 , respectively, each of dimension 2 × 3. In this construction, nodes at the upper level represent matrix rows, nodes...
